prompts: A virus consists of genetic material (DNA or RNA) encased in a protein coat called a capsid, and sometimes an envelope derived from host cell membranes. Infection begins when a virus attaches to a host cell surface receptor, followed by entry through direct penetration, endocytosis, or membrane fusion. Once inside, the viral genome is released, hijacking the host's cellular machinery to replicate its genetic material and produce viral proteins. New virions are assembled and released, often leading to cell lysis or budding, to infect neighboring cells. This replication-assembly-release cycle characterizes viral infection and spread.
